item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No real opinion on the skip itself (not my kind of story) but I fixed a minor spelling mistake

"isn't how is happens" -> "isn't how it happens".

If I've made a mistake and that was supposed to be intentional feel free to revert it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason the final image left in the story, of Husayn feeding and petting a cat while wondering about his own humanity, has struck me hard.

Good job.

item.title: Immersion Killer
item.content:encoded:
I know this is for the Cliche Con, but I think detailing the whole Containment Team killed the immersion from any other entry.

You could've done this on purpose, it just seemed odd to me. +1 for everything else though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
felt like I'd read the main plot - that of Foundation researchers and staff being completely imbibed by an anomaly - way too many times before.

I mean, it is Cliche Con

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god, Tufto, if you delete this I will be so mad >:T

This is so good. I love the mystery. And to be honest, I took something different from it then you laid out here. But it's great either way.

I really want to know what's behind that door now, though…

item.title: Potential Error
item.content:encoded:
"And this isn't how is happens."

Did you mean to say "And this isn't how this happens."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unlike most people here, I actually really like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this. It's obvious from the start that it's Cliche-Con, but I don't necessarily think that's bad.

I did find it sort of…what's the word…mundane? Not exactly boring, as I enjoyed reading it, but not exciting or really turning the cliche on its head. I saw coming the fact that they wouldn't figure it out about a third of the way through, but it didn't bother me or make me stop reading.

All in all, I think it's a good distillation of the cliche and makes for an enjoyable read. I give it a hesitant +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does "the darkness was inside you the whole time" count as a cliche as well? Though here's its very well observed and thought out…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, now that this has (to my great surprise) done well enough to not be deleted, I'd like to write a bit about what I was going for here, since it might not be entirely clear. Don't open this if you don't want to read the author's interpretation of the piece; it's only one way to look at it, after all. Death of the author and all that.

Spoilers
Spoiled!

The plot and anomaly here are, as you can see, very by-the-book. They are essentially just mechanisms to carry the broader thematic points. I very deliberately chose a familiar anomaly/plot structure but also made them seem rote so I could concentrate on the thematic elements I was more interested in- a response/way of using the inherent nature of Cliche-Con. We never find out what's on the other side- there's something there, but it's utterly unknown. It represents that unknown jump, that escape, we'd rather take than face up to the impossible.

This is ultimately a skip about despair in the face of both the unknown and in the face of the basic facts of one's own life. The anomaly is a mystery to be solved, but they can't solve it. They know there's something on the other side, but they have no idea what; their lives, which revolve around this kind of work, become more and more consumed by the thing they study. It's a metaphor, basically, for the thing that draws one in.

The five members of the research team each represent a response or coping mechanism to these things (although they are deliberately impure and do exist as characters beyond these, of course). Summers, being in charge but refusing to accept the failure of her team, represents the ideals the mind places for any project or passion, be it Wiki-writing or academic study or what have you. But as she tries, more and more, to keep everything sane and functioning, she becomes more and more consumed and distorted in her aims, mirroring the mind's potential inability to preserve one's ideals or ideal way of seeing things.

Rafael and Emily represent repression and burnout, respectively. Rafael tries to pretend like things are OK, like his staff's emotional problems are nothing; until eventually he's unable to stop Emily or understand why she is as she is. Emily herself is just pure exhaustion, an expression of what happens when you have responsibilities or needs but can't cope with them any more, willing to go to any lengths just to get away from it all.

Olivia is a representation of the effect on the mind of these problems or mechanisms. She remains efficient, competent, trying to adjust to changing circumstances and do something real and meaningful- but she's ultimately defeated by the sheer weight of the pressure and people around her. She's the closest thing this story has to a hero, and she's the least prominent voice in the whole thing as a result.

Husayn, finally, is the admission of failure, the non-functioning thing created by the others. The pressures put upon him by all of the others, their failure to comprehend, their disappointment, all band together to create a cocktail of such inefficiency that he he'd rather seek escape or oblivion or the sheer terror of the unknown rather than be rescued. He's death itself. Even the narrative his diaries spin of Joaquin's potential disappearance cannot come to anything- the cat just reappears.

Was there a cognitohazard, or not? It doesn't matter. I leave it ambiguous so the reader doesn't take this as a tale about people going mad or people being worked upon anomalously- it's about the decay, the decline, the things that happen when obsession and despair and madness make mockery of us all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, someone actually reads author pages? :P

But yeah, I was not expecting to be referenced here. A pleasant surprise indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Malcolm Lloyd > Mal. Dr. Lloyd >MaliceAforethought

Dr. Lloyd is a recurring character in the author's works and also their self-insert (stated on their author page).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's interesting, but it was quickly evident to me that it was a Cliche-Con entry (which by itself is not necessarily bad) and what the cliches were. Given the setup, I'm not sure it really paid off that well. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me strongly of SCP-3930 with a splash of SCP-3005 and even SCP-2790. Ultimately, I failed to connect to the characters and felt like I'd read the main plot - that of Foundation researchers and staff being completely imbibed by an anomaly - way too many times before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: SCP-4699 is a door to another world.

I'm not a big fan of just leaving the description at just a sentence. It doesn't make sense to leave it at that, and continue it with another paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you enjoyed it :p.

Veronica Noches and Tai Eisenstein are by far the most removed from their usernames, they'll be difficult/nigh-impossible to work out. Malcolm Lloyd requires a little knowledge about the author's body of work to get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, Tufto! It's been great seeing you posting more recently, you make good stuff.

Beryl Liamsson. Captain John Kirby. Clement Tortuga. Veronica Noches. Malcolm Lloyd. Anne Doggett. Tai Eisenstein.

Beryl Liamsson > Berylliam >Weryllium
Captain John Kirby > Captain Kirby
Clement Tortuga > Clem Turtle > KindlyTurtleClem
Anne Doggett > Ann. Dog > DrAnnoyingDog

Behold my powers of deduction. I'll figure out who the others are referring to when they fly into my inbox at 88 mph to holler at me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mama take this badge from me
I can't use it anymore
It's getting dark too dark to see
Feels like I'm knockin' on heaven's door

This SCP is an entry into Cliché-Con 2019, in Category 3.

Anomaly Cliché: "A doorway of any shape to a different dimension/timeline/world". Also potentially "Compulsion effect that makes you do bad things/kills you", depending on one's interpretation.
Story Element Cliché: "D-Class = Disposable Class". Also potentially "An interview in which the anomaly is overly cryptic, followed by the interviewer suffering", depending on one's interpretation.

The title is paraphrased from a line in SCP-3000; it seemed appropriate.

In this day and age, SCPs are often just little mysteries to be solved. We set up an idea, often confusingly, and gradually work the reader back to some terrible, affirming, comedic conclusion. It works well.

We do this in life, too. We place demands on people's heads, and expect them to sort it out, and punish any deviation with cries of weakness, expecting adherence to a framework nobody can comprehend.

This is a story about when it all breaks down.

This is also a coldpost, and the most personal thing I've written for the site. I might delete it, it'll probably be deleted, a lot of people will hate it. And that's OK.
